title: 14_dataloader_parallel_workers.ipynb
descriptionMd:
## Mission: Restore Parallel Data Loading

The training pipeline was profiling at 45% GPU utilization — the GPU kept waiting on the CPU to deliver the next batch. After adding `num_workers=4` to the DataLoader, GPU utilization jumped to 89%. Excellent. The change was merged, deployed to the Windows training server, and crashed on the very first iteration.

The error is a pickling failure thrown deep inside PyTorch's multiprocessing machinery. When DataLoader spawns worker subprocesses, it serializes the Dataset object and everything it holds across the process boundary using `pickle`. Something in this Dataset cannot survive that round-trip. The training code is unchanged from the Linux dev machine — but Linux and Windows use different process start methods, and the difference exposes a latent bug the fork-based spawn was silently hiding.

Find the unpicklable callable and replace it with one that survives pickling on every platform. The fix must not touch `num_workers` — reducing it to zero would just reintroduce the 45% GPU utilization the team just shipped to eliminate.
traceback:
Traceback (most recent call last):
  File "train.py", line 27, in <module>
    for batch in loader:
  File ".../torch/utils/data/dataloader.py", line 631, in __next__
    return self._process_data(data)
  File ".../torch/utils/data/dataloader.py", line 677, in _process_data
    data.reraise()
  File ".../torch/utils/data/_utils/mp.py", line 68, in reraise
    raise self.exc_type(self.msg)
_pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x7f3c82a4f100>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed

options.label: Replace the lambda with a module-level def function so pickle can reference it by name
options.rationale: Pickle serializes callables by recording their module and qualified name, then the receiving process resolves that reference after import. A top-level `def normalize` in `__main__` gets the path `__main__.normalize` — importable, stable, resolvable. A lambda expression carries the name `<lambda>` with no qualified path; pickle cannot construct a reference it can look up on the other side of the process boundary. Changing the syntax from `lambda` to `def` is the minimal, portable fix: the behavior is identical, workers can now deserialize the Dataset on every platform.

options.label: Set num_workers=0 to avoid spawning subprocesses entirely
options.rationale: No subprocess means no pickling, so the loop runs — and GPU utilization drops back to the 45% the team just fixed. With `num_workers=0` every batch is loaded synchronously in the training thread: while the GPU computes the forward/backward pass, no prefetching is happening, so the GPU idles every time data loading takes longer than a compute step. This is a correct debugging technique (confirm the pipeline works before adding workers) but not the fix — the task requires keeping `num_workers=4` and making the callable picklable.

options.label: Force the fork start method so subprocesses inherit the parent's memory and skip pickling
options.resultLog:
RuntimeError: context has already been set

Note: Even when this succeeds on Linux, fork + CUDA is unsafe — forked worker processes that inherit an initialized CUDA context produce non-deterministic deadlocks on real training runs.
options.rationale: Fork copies the parent process into each worker, bypassing pickling and making lambdas "work" — on Linux, on this machine, today. But the original crash was on Windows, where `fork` is unavailable (`set_start_method('fork')` raises `ValueError` there), so this is a platform-specific patch that would fail to land on the target machine. Worse, any CUDA initialization before the fork (a prior `torch.cuda` call, a model moved to GPU) produces silent non-determinism or deadlocks in workers, which is exactly why PyTorch defaults to `spawn`. Fix the callable, not the start method.

hints: When `num_workers > 0`, PyTorch spawns separate processes and sends each one a copy of the Dataset via `pickle`. Pickle serializes functions by recording their importable path. What kind of callable cannot be looked up by import path?

Line 22: `normalize` is defined as a lambda expression. Lambda expressions carry the internal name `<lambda>` and have no stable module-level path that pickle can serialize. Replace it with a `def` at module scope — the behavior is identical and the resulting function is picklable.
explanationMd:
### Why the bug occurs
When `num_workers > 0`, PyTorch's DataLoader spawns worker subprocesses using Python's `multiprocessing`. Before a worker can load batches, it needs a copy of the Dataset object. Python ships that copy across the process boundary using `pickle`, which serializes objects by recording a *reference* — the module path and qualified name — that the receiving process can resolve after importing. A `def` function at module level has a path like `__main__.normalize`; pickle records it, the worker imports it, done. A `lambda` expression carries only the internal name `<lambda>` with no qualified module path. Pickle cannot construct a resolvable reference for it, so it raises `PicklingError`.

On Linux, PyTorch defaults to the `fork` start method, which clones the parent process in memory. Forked workers inherit the parent's in-memory objects directly — the lambda is already in their address space and pickling never happens. That is why the code ran on the Linux dev machine and crashed on Windows, where `fork` is unavailable and `spawn` is the only option. The bug was always there; the platform difference exposed it.

### Why the fix is correct
Replacing `lambda x: ...` with `def normalize(x): ...` at module scope gives the function a stable, importable identity. The body is identical — the behavioral change is zero — but now `pickle.dumps(normalize)` succeeds on every platform and every start method. Any top-level callable would work: a `def`, a class instance with `__call__`, or a `functools.partial` wrapping a top-level function. The lambda restriction is the same rule: picklability requires an importable path.
recruiterReview: Correct fix and the right diagnosis. The key insight — that the same code worked on Linux because `fork` avoids pickling and failed on Windows because `spawn` requires it — shows you traced the bug to the platform contract rather than guessing at the symptom. The `num_workers=0` distractor is the classic "makes the test green, breaks the feature" trap, and you didn't take it. The `fork` distractor is subtler: it would silence the Windows error while introducing latent CUDA deadlocks on any real GPU run — recognizing that as a regression risk rather than a fix reflects solid production instincts. Forward note: for transforms with more state, a module-level class with `__call__` is cleaner than relying on closure over module-level `mean`/`std`. Approved. ✅

tutorial.bodyMd:
## DataLoader Workers and the Pickling Contract

PyTorch's DataLoader accelerates data pipelines by overlapping I/O and preprocessing with GPU computation. Setting `num_workers > 0` spawns that many worker subprocesses, each of which prefetches and preprocesses batches in parallel while the GPU crunches the current one. On modern hardware this can raise GPU utilization from 40–50% to 85–90% — a free throughput gain from a one-line change.

The cost is a contract: everything the worker needs must travel across the process boundary, and Python's `multiprocessing` uses `pickle` as the courier.

### What pickle can and cannot serialize
Pickle serializes objects by recording a *reference* — the object's module path and qualified name — that the receiving process resolves after importing the relevant module. This works for:
- Functions defined with `def` at module level (`__main__.normalize` is importable)
- Class instances whose class is importable (the instance state is pickled inline)
- `functools.partial` objects wrapping a picklable base function

Pickle cannot serialize:
- Lambda expressions — they carry the internal name `<lambda>` with no importable path
- Local functions (functions defined inside other functions) — same problem
- Closures over local variables that themselves are not picklable
- Class instances whose class is defined inside a function

The error message is always the same shape: `_pickle.PicklingError: Can't pickle <function <lambda> at 0x...>: attribute lookup <lambda> on __main__ failed`.

### Why it worked on Linux
PyTorch picks a default multiprocessing start method per platform. On Linux it uses `fork`, which clones the parent process in memory. Forked workers inherit every object already in the parent's address space — including lambdas — and pickling never happens. The bug was present all along; `fork` hid it. On macOS (post-Python 3.8) and Windows, `spawn` is the default: each worker starts fresh, imports the main module, and then receives a pickled copy of the Dataset. Spawn exposes every pickling incompatibility that `fork` was silently absorbing.

### The fix: give the callable a stable identity
Replacing `lambda x: ...` with `def normalize(x): ...` at module scope is the minimal change. The function body is identical; only the binding changes from anonymous to named. The named function has the path `__main__.normalize`, pickle records that path, the worker resolves it after import, and the Dataset arrives intact.

Alternatives that also work:
- A class with `__call__` defined at module scope
- `functools.partial(some_module_level_fn, mean=mean, std=std)`
- Any callable from a third-party library (e.g. `torchvision.transforms.Normalize`)

### The num_workers=0 escape hatch
`num_workers=0` avoids spawning entirely, so pickling never happens and the loop runs. It is a useful diagnostic: if the pipeline works with 0 workers but crashes with 4, the bug is pickling. But it is not a fix — it trades the pickling error for the GPU starvation the workers were hired to prevent. Use it to reproduce and isolate; don't ship it.

### Common variations of this bug
- A `transforms.Compose` list that contains a custom inline lambda — the Compose object is picklable; its contents are not.
- A Dataset that stores a model (e.g. for online feature extraction) with a lambda forward hook attached.
- `multiprocessing.Pool.map` with a lambda argument — same error, different context.
- `functools.partial` wrapping a lambda — the partial pickles fine, but it records a reference to the wrapped lambda, which still cannot be resolved.
- A class-based transform defined inside a function or notebook cell — the class itself is not importable and therefore not picklable.
- Code that works in a Jupyter notebook (which uses fork on Linux) but crashes when refactored into a standalone training script on Windows.
